data.title: Restaurant Brands International Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Restaurant Brands International Inc. (RBI), one of the world's largest quick service restaurant companies, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the competitive quick service restaurant industry. RBI operates over 32,000 restaurants across more than 120 countries and territories, owning and franchising four major brands: Tim Hortons, Burger King, Popeyes, and Firehouse Subs.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenues: $8,406 million, driven by acquisitions and system-wide sales growth across franchisor segments, partially offset by unfavorable FX Impact.

Income from Operations: $2,419 million, increased due to higher segment income and favorable changes in other operating expenses.

Net Income: $1,445 million, decreased primarily due to income tax expense compared to a benefit in the prior year and increased loss on early extinguishment of debt.

Diluted EPS: Not explicitly stated in the provided sections.

Business Highlights

Company Overview: Restaurant Brands International, Inc. (RBI) is one of the world's largest quick service restaurant companies, operating over 32,000 restaurants across more than 120 countries and territories. The company owns and franchises four major brands: Tim Hortons, Burger King, Popeyes, and Firehouse Subs.

Franchise Model: Approximately 95% of RBI's system-wide restaurants are franchised. The company believes that operating a limited number of company-owned restaurants helps in testing new products and operational standards before system-wide implementation.

Brand Performance: Tim Hortons is the largest coffee and baked goods chain in Canada, Burger King is the second-largest quick service hamburger chain globally, Popeyes is the second-largest quick service chicken concept, and Firehouse Subs is a leading player in the sandwich category in North America.

Geographical Performance: As of December 31, 2024, Tim Hortons operates in 22 countries, Burger King in 125, Popeyes in 47, and Firehouse Subs in 7. The international segment, which includes operations outside the U.S. and Canada, showed a system-wide sales growth of 10.0% in 2024.

Sales Units: The total system restaurant count at the end of 2024 was 32,125, with Tim Hortons having 6,043 restaurants, Burger King 19,732, Popeyes 4,979, and Firehouse Subs 1,371.

Operational Strategy: RBI's strategy focuses on three core pillars: Quality, Service, and Convenience. The company aims to enhance guest experience through training, technology, and restaurant reimaging, while also expanding its global footprint.

Future Outlook: RBI plans to increase the pace of remodels for Burger King restaurants acquired in the Carrols Acquisition and accelerate net restaurant growth at Popeyes China and Firehouse Subs Brazil. The company is also working to find new partners for these operations.

Sustainability Initiatives: RBI is committed to sustainability through its 'Restaurant Brands for Good' plan, focusing on food quality, environmental footprint reduction, and community support.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: In 2024, the company completed the acquisition of Carrols Restaurant Group Inc. and Popeyes China, expanding its operational footprint. The company also acquired the remaining equity interests in Burger King China, with plans to find a new controlling shareholder. These acquisitions are part of a broader strategy to optimize costs and leverage global scale while maintaining brand independence.

Capital Management: The company increased its Term Loan B by $750 million to fund the Carrols acquisition and refinanced a portion of this loan with the issuance of $1,200 million of 6.125% First Lien Senior Notes due 2029. Additionally, $500 million of 5.625% First Lien Senior Notes due 2029 were issued to redeem outstanding notes. The company maintained a share repurchase authorization of up to $1,000 million, with $500 million remaining under this authorization as of December 31, 2024. Dividends were increased to $0.62 per common share for the first quarter of 2025.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its strategy of refranchising the majority of Carrols Burger King restaurants and finding new partners for Popeyes China and Firehouse Subs Brazil. It expects capital expenditures to total $400 million to $450 million in 2025, focusing on property and equipment additions. The company also anticipates increased cash taxes due to new Canadian tax legislation and plans to manage its debt obligations through strategic refinancing and interest rate swaps.

Challenges and Risks

Intense Competition: The restaurant industry is intensely competitive, with few barriers to entry. Competitors include independent operators and large chains, which may have greater financial resources and economies of scale, potentially impacting the company's market position.

Brand Value and Consumer Perception: The success of the company's brands depends on maintaining consumer trust and perception. Adverse publicity, health campaigns, or changes in consumer preferences could negatively impact brand value and financial results.

Economic Conditions: Economic downturns, inflation, and rising interest rates can reduce consumer discretionary spending, affecting restaurant sales and profitability.

Unforeseen Events: Natural disasters, pandemics, and geopolitical conflicts can disrupt operations, supply chains, and consumer behavior, leading to financial losses.

Global Operations Risks: Operating in diverse international markets exposes the company to regulatory, economic, and geopolitical risks, including currency fluctuations and compliance with foreign laws.

Supply Chain Vulnerabilities: Increases in food and commodity costs, or supply chain disruptions, could harm profitability. The company relies on a few distributors and suppliers, increasing the risk of supply interruptions.

Franchised Business Model Risks: The company's reliance on franchisees limits control over operations and exposes it to risks related to franchisee performance and financial health.

Labor Challenges: The company and its franchisees face challenges in attracting and retaining qualified employees, which could impact operations and profitability.

Regulatory and Compliance Risks: Changes in regulations, including labor laws and environmental policies, could increase operational costs and impact business strategies.

Information Technology and Cybersecurity: The company is vulnerable to IT system failures and cybersecurity threats, which could disrupt operations and lead to financial and reputational damage.

Indebtedness: High levels of debt could limit financial flexibility and increase vulnerability to economic changes.

Taxation Risks: Changes in tax laws and disputes with tax authorities could impact the company's financial results.

Management Strategies: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by intense competition and economic conditions. The company is focusing on enhancing digital engagement, improving service quality, and expanding its global footprint to mitigate these risks. Strategic acquisitions, such as the Carrols and PLK China acquisitions, are part of the company's growth strategy, but they also introduce additional risks related to integration and operational management.

Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates, which could adversely affect financial results. The company employs risk management strategies, such as geographic diversification and derivative financial instruments, to mitigate these risks, but these strategies may not be fully effective.

SEC Filing: Restaurant Brands International Inc. [ QSR ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: KFORCE INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Kforce Inc., a leading solutions firm specializing in technology, finance, and accounting staffing services, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive and evolving market.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $1,405.3 million, a decrease of 8.3% from $1,531.8 million in 2023, primarily due to macroeconomic uncertainty affecting client investments.
  • Gross Profit: $385.4 million, down from $427.1 million in 2023, with a gross profit margin of 27.4%, a decrease of 50 basis points from the previous year.
  • Income from Operations: $69.7 million, a decline from $87.1 million in 2023, reflecting tighter control on spending amidst revenue declines.
  • Net Income: $50.4 million, a decrease of 17.5% from $61.1 million in 2023, impacted by lower revenue and gross profit levels.
  • Diluted EPS: $2.68, down from $3.13 in 2023, reflecting the overall decline in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: Kforce Inc. focuses on providing technology talent solutions and has streamlined its business to align with this goal.
  • Revenue Segments: The Technology segment represents 92% of Kforce's overall revenues, with the remaining revenue generated by the Finance and Accounting (FA) segment.
  • Consultant Placement: Approximately 18,000 consultants are placed annually with Fortune 500 and other leading companies, highlighting Kforce's significant role in the staffing industry.
  • Geographical Expansion: In 2024, Kforce established a development center in Pune, India, to support project engagements with U.S.-based clients, enhancing its offshore capabilities.
  • Technology Business Performance: Despite a 6.6% revenue decline in 2024, the Technology business remains a critical component of Kforce's operations, providing talent solutions in areas such as systems architecture, data management, cloud engineering, AI, and network security.
  • FA Business Strategy: The FA business has been repositioned to focus on highly skilled assignments, aligning more closely with the Technology segment to reduce susceptibility to technological change and automation.
  • Consultant Employment: The majority of Kforce's consultants are directly employed by the company, which includes domestic and foreign workers, supporting effective management of payroll taxes, benefits, and other labor costs.
  • Industry Position: Kforce is among the largest publicly-traded specialty staffing firms in the U.S., ranking as the sixth largest in technology temporary staffing and the tenth largest in finance and accounting temporary staffing.
  • Market Opportunity: Kforce is focused on expanding its market share in the U.S. technology temporary staffing industry and investing in higher-level technology services and solutions.
  • Strategic Priorities: Kforce's strategic priorities include back-office transformation, integrated strategy efforts, and evolving nearshore and offshore delivery strategies to enhance service offerings and operational efficiency.
  • Future Outlook: Kforce expects 2025 to be the final year of significant investments in strategic priorities, with meaningful returns anticipated in 2026 and beyond. The company is optimistic about leveraging its India development center to enhance service offerings.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: Kforce is prioritizing investments in strategic initiatives, including the implementation of Workday as part of its back-office transformation program, integrated strategy efforts, and the evolution of its nearshore and offshore delivery capabilities. These initiatives aim to enhance technology capabilities to better support clients, consultants, and candidates.
  • Capital Management: Kforce returned $64.7 million to shareholders through open market repurchases totaling $36.5 million and quarterly dividends totaling $28.2 million in 2024. The company reduced its outstanding debt under the Amended and Restated Credit Facility from $41.6 million to $32.7 million. Additionally, Kforce's Board declared and paid dividends of $1.52 per share in 2024, with an increase to $1.56 per share approved for 2025. The company also repurchased 609,000 shares for $36.5 million, with $63.5 million remaining available for further repurchases under the Board-authorized program.
  • Future Outlook: Kforce plans to continue exercising tight discretionary spend control while balancing productivity levels. The company expects to maintain sufficient liquidity for potential acquisitions or other strategic investments, supported by existing cash, operating cash flows, and available borrowings under its credit facility. Kforce anticipates stable Technology Flex gross profit margins and a decline in FA Flex gross profit margins in the first quarter of 2025. The company is also focused on capturing increased market share when the demand environment improves.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive and Fragmented Industry: Kforce operates in a highly competitive and fragmented staffing industry, which poses significant challenges. The company's focus on technology talent solutions increases its exposure to fluctuations in the technology sector.
  • Foreign Operations Risks: The establishment of a development center in Pune, India, introduces additional risks related to managing foreign operations and compliance with foreign laws.
  • Economic Sensitivity: The U.S. professional staffing industry is sensitive to economic fluctuations, and any downturn could reduce demand for Kforce's services.
  • Client Concentration: The concentration of revenues among large clients increases the risk of significant revenue loss if a major client reduces or terminates its business with Kforce.
  • Market Competition: The staffing market's low barriers to entry mean increased competition, potentially leading to pricing pressures.
  • Operational Risks: Kforce's strategic initiatives, such as the implementation of Workday and the expansion of nearshore and offshore capabilities, may divert management's attention and resources, posing operational risks.
  • Cybersecurity Threats: The company is exposed to cybersecurity threats, which could disrupt operations and damage its reputation.
  • Regulatory Risks: Potential changes in immigration laws affecting the availability of skilled foreign workers and the reclassification of independent contractors could increase costs.
  • Macroeconomic Uncertainty: The ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty has led to decreased client investment in digital transformation, impacting Kforce's revenue. The political landscape and geopolitical risks, such as Middle East tensions and global supply chain disruptions, add to the uncertainty.
  • Market Risks: Kforce is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations that could affect borrowing costs under its credit facility. The use of third-party software and AI introduces operational and compliance risks, as these technologies may not fully align with Kforce's business needs or regulatory requirements.

SEC Filing: KFORCE INC [ KFRC ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: AGNC Investment Corp. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
AGNC Investment Corp., a leading provider of private capital to the U.S. housing market, has released its 2024 10-K report, showcasing significant improvements in financial performance, strategic initiatives, and operational highlights. The company, which primarily invests in Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) on a leveraged basis, operates as a real estate investment trust (REIT) and focuses on generating favorable long-term stockholder returns.

Financial Highlights

  • Interest Income: $2,949 million, reflecting an increase from $2,041 million in 2023, driven by higher yields on investment securities.
  • Net Income: $863 million, a significant increase from $155 million in 2023, indicating improved profitability.
  • Net Income (Loss) Available (Attributable) to Common Stockholders: $731 million, compared to $32 million in 2023, highlighting a substantial improvement in earnings for common shareholders.
  • Net Income (Loss) Per Common Share - Diluted: $0.93, up from $0.05 in 2023, showing enhanced earnings per share.
  • Comprehensive Income (Loss) Available (Attributable) to Common Stockholders: $657 million, compared to $187 million in 2023, reflecting overall improved financial performance.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: AGNC Investment Corp. is a leading provider of private capital to the U.S. housing market, primarily investing in Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) on a leveraged basis. The company operates as a real estate investment trust (REIT) and is internally managed, focusing on generating favorable long-term stockholder returns with a substantial yield component.
  • Investment Strategy: AGNC employs an active management strategy that is dynamic and responsive to evolving market conditions. The company focuses on maintaining a portfolio predominantly consisting of Agency RMBS, capitalizing on discrepancies in the relative valuations in the Agency and non-Agency securities market, and managing various risks including financing, interest rate, prepayment, extension, and credit risks.
  • Targeted Investments: The company primarily invests in Agency RMBS, which are considered a cornerstone of the U.S. financial system. AGNC also invests in non-Agency securities such as Credit Risk Transfer (CRT) securities, non-Agency RMBS, and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS).
  • Financing Strategy: AGNC funds its investments primarily through collateralized borrowings structured as repurchase agreements. The company also utilizes TBA dollar roll transactions to finance the acquisition of Agency RMBS, which provides a form of off-balance sheet financing.
  • Risk Management: As a levered investor in fixed income securities, AGNC employs a variety of investment and risk management strategies to reduce exposure to market risks. The company continuously monitors and adjusts its hedge portfolio, net duration, and leverage to optimize returns over the long term.
  • Regulatory Compliance: AGNC operates to qualify as a REIT under the Internal Revenue Code and remains exempt from regulation under the Investment Company Act. The company must meet specific asset and income tests to maintain its REIT status.
  • Human Capital Management: AGNC emphasizes the strength, wellness, and dedication of its workforce, consisting of 53 full-time employees as of December 31, 2024. The company offers a competitive benefits package and maintains a low voluntary employee turnover rate.
  • Workplace Culture and Ethics: AGNC promotes a corporate culture of open communication, fair treatment, and high ethical standards. The company conducts regular compliance training and maintains policies against discrimination and harassment.
  • Employee Development: AGNC supports professional development through various programs, including professional certification reimbursement, one-on-one coaching, and a formal mentoring program.
  • Inclusion and Diversity: AGNC strives for an inclusive work environment, with 40% of its employees being women and 32% ethnically diverse as of December 31, 2024.
  • Compensation and Benefits: The company offers a pay-for-performance compensation philosophy, including base salary, annual cash bonuses, and long-term equity incentives. AGNC also provides comprehensive medical coverage and a 401(k) Savings Plan with company matching contributions.
  • Market Conditions and Outlook: In 2024, AGNC benefited from a favorable market environment as the Federal Reserve lowered short-term rates. The company anticipates a favorable outlook for Agency RMBS in 2025, with expectations of wide spreads relative to benchmark rates and potential increases in bank demand.
  • Operational Performance: AGNC's investment portfolio increased to $73.3 billion as of December 31, 2024, with a significant portion consisting of Agency RMBS. The company maintained an average leverage ratio of 7.2x tangible stockholders' equity in 2024.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: AGNC Investment Corp. has focused on enhancing its liquidity and capital resources by leveraging its investment portfolio, primarily through collateralized borrowings structured as repurchase agreements and TBA dollar roll transactions. The company has also raised $2.0 billion of common stock through its at-the-market offering program, which was executed at a premium to tangible net book value, thereby generating meaningful book value accretion for common stockholders.
  • Capital Management: AGNC's capital management activities include maintaining a leverage ratio between six and twelve times the amount of tangible stockholders' equity. As of December 31, 2024, the tangible net book value 'at risk' leverage ratio was 7.2x. The company has also focused on managing its repurchase agreement funding position through diversification of counterparties, maintaining a suitable maturity profile, and utilizing interest rate hedging strategies. Additionally, AGNC declared dividends totaling $1.44 per common share for the year 2024.
  • Future Outlook: Looking forward, AGNC anticipates a favorable outlook for Agency mortgage-backed securities in 2025, expecting Agency RMBS spreads relative to benchmark rates to remain wide, offering attractive return opportunities. The company plans to continue its active management strategy, which is dynamic and responsive to evolving market conditions, and aims to maintain adequate levels of liquidity and capital resources to support its operations and strategic objectives.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market and Operational Risks: AGNC operates primarily in the Agency RMBS market, which is subject to various risks including interest rate fluctuations, prepayment risks, and regulatory changes. The company's strategy involves leveraging investments, which inherently increases exposure to market volatility and financing risks. The company's reliance on short-term borrowings through repurchase agreements and TBA dollar roll transactions introduces additional liquidity and funding risks.
  • Investment and Portfolio Management Risks: AGNC faces significant risks related to its investment and portfolio management activities, including spread risk, interest rate and spread volatility, and the impact of the Federal Reserve's actions in the Agency mortgage market. The company's active portfolio management strategy may expose it to greater losses compared to passive strategies. Additionally, changes in prepayment rates and the fair value of assets can adversely affect financial conditions.
  • Financing and Hedging Risks: The company also faces risks related to its financing and hedging activities, such as the potential inability to procure or renew funding on favorable terms, increased borrowing costs, and margin calls that could force asset sales under adverse conditions.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks include potential changes to the Federal conservatorship of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, which could impact the value and liquidity of Agency RMBS. The company must also comply with REIT requirements, which could limit its ability to hedge effectively and may require it to liquidate or forgo attractive investment opportunities.
  • Management's Discussion and Analysis: Management highlights the challenges posed by market volatility and the need to adapt investment strategies accordingly. The company actively monitors market conditions and adjusts its portfolio and hedging strategies to mitigate risks. However, there is no assurance that these measures will be sufficient to offset the negative effects of volatility on the business. The company also emphasizes the importance of maintaining compliance with REIT and Investment Company Act requirements to avoid adverse tax consequences and regulatory restrictions.
  • Market Risk Disclosures: AGNC is exposed to market risks, including interest rate risk, prepayment risk, and credit risk. The company's hedging strategies are designed to mitigate some of these risks, but they may not fully protect against all adverse changes. The effectiveness of hedging strategies can be impacted by factors such as the cost of hedges, correlation between hedge benchmarks and interest rate risks, and regulatory changes affecting derivative transactions. The company also faces risks related to the valuation of its investments, which may not reflect their realizable value in the event of forced liquidation.

SEC Filing: AGNC Investment Corp. [ AGNC ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: ABBOTT LABORATORIES SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories, a global healthcare company known for its diversified portfolio in diagnostics, medical devices, nutritional products, and established pharmaceuticals, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a highly regulated industry.

Financial Highlights

Abbott Laboratories reported strong financial results for 2024, showcasing significant growth across key metrics:

  • Net Sales: $41,950 million, reflecting a 4.6% increase from 2023, driven by higher sales in the Medical Devices, Established Pharmaceutical Products, and Nutritional Products segments, partially offset by a decrease in demand for COVID-19 tests.
  • Gross Profit: $20,366 million, representing 50.9% of net sales, up from 50.3% in 2023 due to favorable impacts of margin improvement initiatives.
  • Operating Earnings: $6,825 million, an increase from $6,478 million in 2023, driven by higher sales volumes and margin improvement initiatives.
  • Net Earnings: $13,402 million, significantly up from $5,723 million in 2023, primarily due to a $7.5 billion non-cash valuation allowance adjustment related to deferred tax assets.
  • Basic Earnings Per Common Share: $7.67, compared to $3.28 in 2023, reflecting the substantial increase in net earnings.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: $7.64, up from $3.26 in 2023, consistent with the increase in net earnings.

Business Highlights

Abbott's diverse business segments and geographical reach contributed to its strong operational performance in 2024:

  • Revenue Segments: Abbott operates through four main segments: Established Pharmaceutical Products, Diagnostic Products, Nutritional Products, and Medical Devices. Each segment contributes to the company's diverse healthcare product offerings.
  • Geographical Performance: Sales in emerging markets, which represent approximately 37% of total company sales, increased by 8.2% in 2024, driven by higher revenue in Latin America, Southeast Asia, and the Middle East.
  • New Product Launches: In 2024, Abbott launched several new products, including the Lingo® and Libre Rio™ CGM systems, the Esprit™ BTK system, and the TriClip® for tricuspid regurgitation. These innovations reflect Abbott's commitment to expanding its product portfolio.
  • Sales Units: Abbott's continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) systems saw significant growth, with sales totaling $6.4 billion in 2024, reflecting a 21.8% increase over the previous year.
  • Future Outlook: Abbott plans to continue investing in product development across its segments, focusing on areas such as diagnostics, medical devices, and nutritional products to drive sustainable growth.
  • Operational Efficiency: Abbott implemented margin improvement initiatives in 2024, which positively impacted its operating margin profile, despite challenges such as foreign exchange and inflation.
  • Segment Performance: The Medical Devices segment experienced double-digit growth in 2024, particularly in Diabetes Care, Structural Heart, Electrophysiology, and Heart Failure, driven by new product launches and increased procedure volumes.
  • Regulatory Developments: Abbott continues to navigate complex regulatory environments, including the European Union's Medical Devices Regulation and In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation, which require significant technical expertise and investment to ensure compliance.

Strategic Initiatives

Abbott's strategic initiatives and capital management activities in 2024 focused on enhancing its product portfolio and maintaining financial strength:

  • Strategic Acquisitions: Abbott focused on strategic acquisitions to enhance its product portfolio, including the acquisition of Bigfoot Biomedical, Inc. to advance diabetes management solutions and Cardiovascular Systems, Inc. to expand its vascular device offerings.
  • Capital Management: Abbott maintained a strong capital management strategy by repurchasing approximately 10.2 million shares for $1.1 billion in 2024 under the 2021 repurchase program. The company declared dividends of $2.24 per share in 2024, reflecting a 7.7% increase from the previous year, and authorized a new $7 billion share repurchase program. Abbott also managed its debt effectively, repaying significant portions of its long-term debt, including the €590 million and $1.05 billion notes upon maturity, and modified its yen-denominated term loan to extend maturity and increase principal.
  • Future Outlook: Abbott plans to continue investing in product development to sustain growth, focusing on expanding its diagnostics, medical devices, and nutritional product lines. The company aims to maintain its market-leading position in key areas and explore new opportunities in emerging markets. Abbott also intends to manage its capital efficiently, ensuring that cash flow from operations exceeds capital expenditures and dividends, while maintaining an investment-grade credit rating.

Challenges and Risks

Abbott faces several challenges and risks that could impact its business operations and financial performance:

  • Regulatory Environment: Abbott operates in a highly regulated environment, with its products subject to comprehensive government regulation by the U.S. FDA and similar international agencies. Compliance with these regulations is costly and affects Abbott's business significantly. Changes in healthcare regulations, such as the introduction of the Medical Devices Regulation in the EU, increase the complexity and cost of compliance.
  • Operational Risks: Abbott faces several business and operational risks, including disruptions to its global supply chain, which could negatively affect its results. The company also depends on sophisticated information systems, making it susceptible to cybersecurity incidents that could have a material adverse effect on its business. Additionally, Abbott's research and development efforts may not succeed, potentially causing revenue and profitability to decline.
  • Economic and Geopolitical Risks: Economic, geopolitical, and industry risks include cost containment efforts that could reduce future revenues and operating income, as well as fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates that have adversely affected Abbott's financial statements. The expiration or loss of intellectual property protection and licenses may also impact future revenues and operating income. Furthermore, public health crises, such as widespread outbreaks of infectious diseases, pose a risk to Abbott's operations and financial condition.
  • Market Risks: Abbott is exposed to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which may adversely affect its financial results. The company enters into hedging arrangements to mitigate some of its foreign currency exposure, but cannot predict changes in exchange rates or its ability to mitigate these risks. Additionally, adverse changes in tax laws, regulations, or interpretations could have a material adverse effect on Abbott's effective tax rate, financial condition, and results of operations.

SEC Filing: ABBOTT LABORATORIES [ ABT ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: US Stocks Dive as Economic Data and Inflation Fears Spark Market Selloff
data.description:
US stocks plunged on Friday as economic data raised concerns about a slowing US economy and persistent inflation, prompting investors to seek safer assets.

The S&P 500 lost 1.7%, and the Nasdaq 100 slipped 2.1%, while the Dow Jones plunged 748 points, marking its biggest loss of the year.

UnitedHealth shares plunged 7.2% following reports that the Department of Justice is investigating its Medicare billing practices, making it the worst-performing Dow component.

Consumer sentiment also took a hit, with the University of Michigan’s index falling to 64.7, reflecting growing concerns over inflation, which consumers now expect to rise to 4.3% in the coming year.

The slide followed Walmart's (-2.5%) disappointing outlook and broader fears about the impact of President Trump's tariff policies.

On the week, the S&P 500 dropped by 1.6%, while the Dow and Nasdaq slipped 2.5% and 2.4%, respectively.

data.title: TSX Falls as Mixed Data Clouds BoC Outlook
data.description:
The S&P/TSX Composite Index dropped 1.4% to close at 25,147 on Friday, bringing its weekly loss to 1.3% as investors assessed mixed economic data that cast uncertainty over the Bank of Canada’s policy trajectory.

A 0.4% decline in January retail sales, the first drop in seven months, signaled softening consumer spending after December’s strong surge, raising concerns about economic momentum.

Meanwhile, inflationary pressures remained strong, with industrial producer prices rising 1.6% and the Raw Materials Price Index jumping 3.7% month-over-month, highlighting the BoC’s challenge in balancing growth and price stability while tempering expectations for further easing.

Most equities traded flat to negative, but commodity producers saw sharp declines amid declining oil and gold prices.

Energy giants Canadian Natural, Suncor, Imperial Oil, and Cenovus lost between 1.3% and 4.2%, while major miners Agnico Eagle, Barrick Gold, Wheaton Precious, and Franco-Nevada shed 1.6% to 2.3%.

data.title: Market Chatter: JPMorgan, Barclays Poised to Advise Cineworld on Potential IPO, Merger
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase and Barclays are likely contenders to become advisers on Cineworld Group's potential initial public offering or merger considerations, Bloomberg reported Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

Cineworld is evaluating an IPO for its business outside the UK, including a theater chain under the Regal brand in the US and Cinema City in Eastern Europe and Israel, the people reportedly told Bloomberg.

Cineworld's advisers may also consider potential mergers with competitors such as AMC Entertainment and Cinemark , the report added.

Barclays declined to comment, while JPMorgan, AMC, Cinemark and Cineworld did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' requests for comment.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Penumbra, Inc. Announces Don Kassing will Retire from Board of Directors
data.description:
Penumbra, Inc. Announces Don Kassing will Retire from Board of Directors

PR Newswire

ALAMEDA, Calif., Feb. 21, 2025

ALAMEDA, Calif., Feb. 21, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Penumbra, Inc. , the world's leading thrombectomy company, announced today that after 17 years of distinguished service, Don Kassing has notified the company that he will retire from the board of directors effective April 1, 2025. Kassing joined Penumbra's Board of Directors in 2008 and has served as Presiding Director since 2015.

"We are grateful for Don's many contributions to the board over the years, particularly his wisdom and guidance in complex situations and through our phases of growth," said Adam Elsesser, board chair, president and chief executive officer of Penumbra. "We also thank Don for his steadfast leadership and dedication to helping Penumbra advance our mission of helping more and more people over the years."

Mr. Kassing is President Emeritus of San Jose State University. Mr. Kassing served as President of San Jose State University from 2004 to 2008, Interim President from 2010 to 2011, and Vice President, Administration and Finance and Chief Financial Officer from 1993 to 2004. Prior to his tenure at San Jose State University, Mr. Kassing spent 18 years in higher education and 11 years in private industry, including eight years in merchandising/marketing and operations management at Caleres, Inc. (formerly known as the Brown Group, Inc.), a leading footwear retailer and wholesaler, and spent 3 years in corporate finance at the General Motors Corporation. Mr. Kassing received a B.A. and an M.B.A. from Saint Louis University.

About Penumbra

Penumbra, Inc., the world's leading thrombectomy company, is focused on developing the most innovative technologies for challenging medical conditions such as ischemic stroke, venous thromboembolism such as pulmonary embolism, and acute limb ischemia. Our broad portfolio, which includes computer assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT), centers on removing blood clots from head-to-toe with speed, safety and simplicity. By pioneering these innovations, we support healthcare providers, hospitals and clinics in more than 100 countries, working to improve patient outcomes and quality of life. For more information, visit www.penumbrainc.com and connect on Instagram, LinkedIn and X.

Investor Relations

Penumbra, Inc.

investors@penumbrainc.com

HEI Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2024 Results
data.description:
(21:05 GMT) *Hawaiian Electric 4Q Loss/Shr 40c >HE

(21:05 GMT) Press Release: HEI Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2024 Results

HEI Reports Fourth Quarter and Full Year 2024 Results

  • Favorable Hawaii Supreme Court Decision Provides Clarity Needed to Help Finalize Maui Tort Litigation Settlement
  • Strong Execution on Strategic Priorities in a Pivotal Year
    -- Definitive Settlement Agreements Reached in Maui Wildfire Tort
    Litigation
    -- Sale of 90.1% of American Savings Bank Simplified HEI's Strategy
    and Regulatory Position While Allowing Enhanced Focus on Utility Business; Proceeds Will Be Used to Reduce Debt
    -- Rapid Implementation of Utility Wildfire Mitigation Efforts: Grid
    Hardening and Redesign, Improved Situational Awareness and Operational Practices, and Enhanced Stakeholder Engagement Efforts Implemented to Reduce Risk
    -- Utility Achieved a 36% Renewable Portfolio Standard in 2024,
    Accelerating Progress Toward the 2030 Milestone of 40%
    -- Typical Residential Bill Decreased 7% in 2024; Utility Returned
    $18 Million in Bill Credits to Customers

HONOLULU--(BUSINESS WIRE)--February 21, 2025--

Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. (NYSE - HE) (HEI) today reported a net loss for the full year 2024 of $1,426 million, or $11.23 per share, compared to net income of $199 million, or $1.81 per share in 2023. Excluding the impacts of discontinued operations, Maui wildfire-related expenses and the Pacific Current asset impairment recorded in the third quarter, Core(1) income from continuing operations was $124 million, or $0.98 per share, compared to $152 million, or $1.38 per share in 2023.

The fourth quarter 2024 net loss was $68 million, or $0.40 per share, compared to net income of $49 million, or $0.44 per share, in the fourth quarter of 2023. Core income from continuing operations was $35 million for the fourth quarter of 2024 compared to $37 million in the fourth quarter of 2023.

"The past year was pivotal in our company's history, and I am proud of the significant progress we've made to address the challenges before us and build a foundation for long-term success," said Scott Seu, HEI president and CEO.

"Over the course of the year, we achieved numerous milestones in our efforts to regain HEI's financial strength and emerge a stronger, more resilient company best positioned to serve our communities for the long term. The settlement agreements signed in November, along with the favorable Hawaii Supreme Court decision issued earlier this month, allow us to move forward with a clearer line of sight toward resolution of the Maui wildfire tort litigation. The sale of over 90% of American Savings Bank in December simplifies our strategy and regulatory position while allowing us to reduce holding company debt and focus more on our core utility business. The utility continued to rapidly progress its wildfire mitigation efforts throughout the year, and operational changes, new technology and the Public Safety Power Shutoff program implemented in 2024 have led to substantial strides in reducing risk of ignition from utility equipment. We also saw significant progress on another key strategic initiative, with the utility reaching a 36% renewable portfolio standard in 2024. This puts us on track to meet the 40% by 2030 milestone significantly ahead of schedule. These collective actions position our company well as we continue our commitment to a stronger, more resilient and more financially healthy future."

As previously disclosed, on February 10, 2025 the Hawaii Supreme Court issued a decision regarding the reserved questions posed to them by the Second Circuit Court. The Hawaii Supreme Court's decision clarifies that, once the settlement becomes final, insurers seeking to recover amounts paid to settling plaintiffs cannot separately sue defendants, including for amounts beyond the settlement agreed to by the plaintiffs and defendants. The Court's decision aligns with the Company's and plaintiffs' positions on key questions that arose from insurers' challenges to the settlement agreements reached in the Maui wildfire tort litigation.

HAWAIIAN ELECTRIC COMPANY (HAWAIIAN ELECTRIC) EARNINGS(2)

Full Year Results:

Hawaiian Electric's full-year net loss was $1,226 million, compared to net income of $194 million in 2023, with the decrease primarily driven by the following items:

  • $1,875 million ($1,392 million after-tax) loss due to the accrual of estimated wildfire liabilities from tort-related legal claims and cross claims as of December 31, 2024 (net of insurance recoveries);
  • $76 million ($56 million after taxes) in higher operations and maintenance (O&M) expenses, driven principally by the settlement of indemnification claims asserted by the state, higher wildfire mitigation program expenses and higher property and general liability insurance costs; and
  • $7 million ($6 million after taxes) of higher depreciation expense.

These items were partially offset by the following:

  • $43 million ($29 million after taxes) higher revenues, including $25 million from the annual revenue adjustment mechanism, $7 million from the major project interim recovery mechanism, $6 million of demand response program revenues (offset by expenses included in O&M) and $5 million from performance incentive mechanisms;
  • $4 million ($3 million after taxes) of lower interest expense; and
  • $3 million ($2 million after taxes) from a gain on sale of property.

Hawaiian Electric's Core net income for 2024 was $181 million. Pre-tax wildfire-related expenses of $2,019 million were partially offset by $86 million in insurance recoveries and $38 million of costs deferred pursuant to the Public Utilities Commission's decision allowing Hawaiian Electric to defer these costs.

Fourth Quarter Results:

Hawaiian Electric's net income for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $46 million, compared to $58 million in the fourth quarter of 2023, with the variance driven by the following items: $30 million ($25 million after taxes) of higher O&M, $13 million ($9 million after taxes) in higher revenues, $3 million ($2 million after taxes) from a gain on sale of property and a $3 million ($2 million after taxes) impact from better heat rate performance. Hawaiian Electric's Core net income was approximately $49 million for both the fourth quarters of 2024 and 2023.

Utility Dividend Update

The utility dividend to HEI continues to be suspended, as holding company cash needs are limited following HEI's recent equity issuance and the continued suspension of the dividend to HEI's common equity shareholders.

DISCONTINUED OPERATIONS - AMERICAN SAVINGS BANK (ASB)

As previously announced, on December 31, 2024 HEI, ASB and ASB Hawaii (ASB's parent holding company) closed on the sale of 90.1% of the common stock of ASB to various investors. Accordingly, the results of ASB are presented as discontinued operations in the consolidated financial statements. For the full year 2024, the loss from discontinued operations totaled $103 million, compared to net income of $53 million in 2023. Excluding wildfire expenses, the goodwill impairment recorded in the second quarter, and the net loss recorded in accordance with the December 2024 sale transaction, Core income for 2024 was $79 million.

HOLDING AND OTHER COMPANIES

The holding and other companies' net loss was $96 million in 2024 compared to $48 million in 2023. The higher net loss for the year was primarily due to the Pacific Current asset impairment recorded in the third quarter, higher wildfire-related expenses and higher expenses at Pacific Current. Core net loss for the year was $56 million compared to $43 million in 2023. The fourth quarter 2024 net loss was $17 million compared to $13 million in the fourth quarter of 2023. The higher net loss compared to the prior year quarter was primarily due to lower Pacific Current net income. Core net loss for the fourth quarter of 2024 was $14 million compared to $12 million in the fourth quarter of 2023.

HEI and Hawaiian Electric Company, Inc. (Hawaiian Electric) intend to continue to use HEI's website, www.hei.com, as a means of disclosing additional information; such disclosures will be included in the Investor Relations section of the website. Accordingly, investors should routinely monitor the Investor Relations section of HEI's website, in addition to following HEI's and Hawaiian Electric's press releases, HEI's and Hawaiian Electric's Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) filings and HEI's public conference calls and webcasts. ABOUT HEI

The HEI family of companies provides the energy services that empower much of the economic and community activity of Hawaii. HEI's electric utility, Hawaiian Electric, supplies power to approximately 95% of Hawaii's population and is undertaking an ambitious effort to decarbonize its operations and the broader state economy, and modernize and harden the grid to ensure resilience and public safety. HEI also helps advance Hawaii's sustainability goals through investments by its non-regulated subsidiary, Pacific Current. For more information, visit www.hei.com.

HEI management uses certain non-GAAP measures to evaluate the performance of HEI. Core earnings and other financial measures as presented here may not be comparable to similarly titled measures used by other companies. The reconciling adjustments from GAAP earnings to Core earnings are limited to the costs related to the Maui wildfires, costs related to the strategic review and majority sale of ASB, and the asset impairment taken in connection with HEI's ongoing review of strategic options for Pacific Current. Sector Update: Health Care Stocks Softer Late Afternoon
data.description:
Health care stocks were decreasing late Friday afternoon, with the NYSE Health Care Index easing 0.2% and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) shedding 0.3%.

The iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) was fractionally lower.

In corporate news, Estrella Immunopharma shares rose 2% after the firm said it has completed the first dose group in an ongoing phase 1/2 trial assessing EB103 in adults with relapsed/refractory B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

Bluebird bio shares plunged 42% after it agreed to be acquired by funds managed by Carlyle (CG) and SK Capital Partners.

UnitedHealth Group on Friday refuted a report by The Wall Street Journal that the US Department of Justice started an investigation into the health insurer's Medicare billing practices. UnitedHealth shares fell 7.3%.

Novo Nordisk resolved its shortage of weight-loss drugs Ozempic and Wegovy, according to an update on the US Food and Drug Administration website. Companies such as Hims & Hers Health will no longer be legally allowed to make and sell compounded alternatives. Novo Nordisk shares jumped 5.4%, while Hims & Hers tumbled 24%.

data.title: Zeta Global to Post Strong Q4 Results, RBC Says
data.description:
Zeta Global is expected to post strong Q4 results backed by "healthy" advertising and marketing budgets, RBC Capital Markets said Friday in a report.

Investors probably will focus on fiscal 2025 guidance and a potential long-term plan through fiscal 2028, RBC said.

A revenue target of $2 billion in revenue by fiscal 2028 implies a 20% compound annual growth rate, "the most likely number," the report said.

Zeta is "well positioned" for the evolving total addressable market, driven by artificial intelligence "unlocking the value of customer data," RBC said.

Shares of the company had dropped after a short-seller report, prompting the company to hold a data summit to clarify collection and usage to address investor concerns, the report said.

RBC maintained an outperform rating on Zeta's stock with a $40 price target. Results from Q4 are expected Tuesday.

Shares of the company fell 7.3% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: Treasury Yields Fall Ahead of PCE Inflation Week — Market Talk
data.description:
1557 ET - Treasury yields decline ahead of a data-heavy week that will include the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. January's PCE index is due Friday and economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expect the 12-month reading to slow to 2.4% from December's 2.6%. Core is forecast to decline to 2.6% from 2.8%. PCE will be preceded by durable goods orders and a second 4Q GDP estimate, both on Thursday. Housing data are due earlier in the week. An unexpected decline on U.S. composite PMI weakened yields today. The 10-year declines 0.057 percentage point, its fourth consecutive weekly loss, to 4.419%. The two-year falls 0.066 p.p. to 4.192%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1522 ET - Market-based estimates of inflation over the next 12 months have increased, data published by Barclays show. The rate on 1-year CPI swaps--a way for traders to bet on how they think the consumer-price index will change over roughly the next 12 months--has risen to around 3%, up from less than 2% six months ago. Since CPI came in at 3% in January, it's a sign that traders are skeptical inflation will make any further progress lower at all this year. That rhymes with the outlook from the Fed, whose officials penciled in 2.5% year-end PCE inflation for 2025 in December. Analysts are expecting PCE inflation to come in at about 2.5% for January--so the Fed estimates too suggest the possibility of no further inflation progress this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1515 ET - JPMorgan Chase's brick-and-mortar expansion promises to drive more dominance for the bank, Truist analysts say. JPM has by far the most branches younger than 10 years old (around 1,100 vs. roughly 500 at Bank of America, which is closest behind), as it has spread to the entire continental U.S. over the past 7 years. It generally takes more than 5 years for bank deposits at new branches to meet the level of mature ones, so JPM has a long runway of opportunity, which analysts estimate could be around 15% in retail deposit growth. The bank is still expanding, with 500 new builds planned by 2027. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1405 ET - The outlook for investment in corporate credit shows some bullish signs, says Dan Genter, of Genter Capital. "There isn't a lot of pressure on earnings...and the momentum is good, cash-flow is strong," he says. His firm traditionally prefers investment-grade credit, but Genter also likes lower-rated corporations likely to be upgraded. He says he usually has 60% of his fixed-income portfolio in corporates and 40% in Treasurys, but it has recently inverted as spreads narrowed to historic lows. "We took profits in some of the individual corporates, where spreads narrowed, and use that to put more into the belly of the curve of the Treasurys." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1402 ET - Trade wars could hurt some sectors that fixed-income investors should avoid, Genter Capital's Dan Genter says. He believes that, eventually, tariffs will be more of a negotiation tool and not as massive as currently feared. But markets will be driven by perception, punishing industries perceived as potential victims of reciprocal tariffs from U.S. trade partners. "Consumer durable and non-durable products are going to be the most affected," he says. The financial sector, in turn, "is very unlikely to be affected." An exception would be financial companies that are very exposed to commercial real estate, Genter says. He also like credit from utilities, due to increasing demand for power. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1400 ET - Ten-year and longer Treasury yields are likely to rise, Genter Capital's Dan Genter says. He doesn't forecast much inflation as a result of tariffs, while he expects the economy to grow. "If we have more inflation, then it's going to justify even more steepening to the curve," he says. Genter expects the 10-year to reach 5%, up from 4.5% now. That outlook underlines his strategy to stay away from long-term bonds. "To us, it is not worth the risk to be," in the long-end of the yield curve, he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1359 ET - With uncertainties mounting around Fed policy and tariffs, fixed-income requires caution, Dan Genter, of Genter Capital, says. He likes Treasurys with maturities up to seven years. "We are not going to push too much far out into the curve." Long-term bond prices, which fell as yields rose in recent months, might look attractive, but "it's too unclear, with regards to the direction of the tail of the curve," Genter says. The uncertainty basically stems from lack of clarity on how tariffs and other Trump policies will affect inflation. Genter expects the inflationary impact to be minimal, but until it becomes more clear to investors "there is no direction at all." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1355 ET - Booking Holdings blew past expectations in 4Q, fueled by global healthy travel demand and the continued execution of its strategic initiatives, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note. The analysts expect the parent of Booking.com and Kayak to continue the run in 2025, in part citing the company's growing direct and loyalty bookings, as well as its lean into generative AI. These efforts should drive multi-year double-digit percent EPS growth, they write. Booking says demand is continuing to hold up so far in 2025, and that it will lean more heavily into social media marketing, particularly in partnership with Meta Platforms. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1349 ET - Desjardins Group economist Royce Mendes says Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem's speech confirms the BOC would cut rates should President Trump impose 25% tariffs on Canadian nonenergy imports, and 10% levy on Canadian energy products. Mendes cites one sentence in the speech, in which Macklem says that cuts could help smooth the adjustment to tariffs "provided the inflationary impact of tariffs is not too big." Mendes says this is in line with Desjardins' view that BOC would cut its policy rate by a half- to full-percentage point more than otherwise should hefty US tariffs materialize. In a Q&A session with a suburban Toronto audience after his speech, Macklem cautions BOC can't allow a "tariff problem to become an inflation problem." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1257 ET - Celsius' $1.8 billion buy of Alani Nu should complement the company, William Blair analysts say in a research note, citing the rapidly growing energy drink brand's appeal to female consumers, as well as its relevance with Gen Z and Millennial consumers. The deal, which is expected to be accretive to per-share earnings within a year, will also alleviate competition risks, they add. The acquisition comes as Celsius says it is confident in its prospects for 2025, despite not providing earnings guidance. The company is launching new flavors, expanding into new categories and seeking to increase its distribution, which the analysts say should add to its already attractive position. Celsius surges 26% following a 4Q earnings beat. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1249 ET - AppLovin remains the clear leader in mobile game advertising, even after Unity debuted an AI-driven ad platform this week, Benchmark analyst Mike Hickey says. Unity on Thursday announced the launch of Vector, a self-learning ad platform aimed at improving ad performance on mobile games. AppLovin's stock is off 14% since Wednesday's close as investors worry about tightening competition, but Hickey says it is still much further along in development than Unity. Vector is set to roll out in 1Q and is effectively a start-over in ad monetization, Hickey says, while AppLovin is already monetizing and scaling its platform. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1238 ET - While the demand recovery timeline in furniture and furnishings has taken a lot longer than anticipated, the risk/reward set-up on Wayfair remains very compelling, Raymond James analysts say in a research note. The online furniture retailer continues to outperform its peers and has created a leaner P&L to drive meaningful Ebitda growth as demand returns. "We believe Wayfair has the right playbook," the analysts say, adding that the furniture industry is positioned better to modestly improve throughout 2025 after multiple years of pressure. Raymond James raises its 1Q adjusted Ebitda estimate to $81 million from $73 million, but lowers its FY25 adjusted Ebitda views to $474 million from $481 million previously on a 0.8% increase in annual revenue growth. Shares fall 6.7% to $43.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1557 ET - Treasury yields decline ahead of a data-heavy week that will include the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. January's PCE index is due Friday and economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expect the 12-month reading to slow to 2.4% from December's 2.6%. Core is forecast to decline to 2.6% from 2.8%. PCE will be preceded by durable goods orders and a second 4Q GDP estimate, both on Thursday. Housing data are due earlier in the week. An unexpected decline on U.S. composite PMI weakened yields today. The 10-year declines 0.057 percentage point, its fourth consecutive weekly loss, to 4.419%. The two-year falls 0.066 p.p. to 4.192%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1522 ET - Market-based estimates of inflation over the next 12 months have increased, data published by Barclays show. The rate on 1-year CPI swaps--a way for traders to bet on how they think the consumer-price index will change over roughly the next 12 months--has risen to around 3%, up from less than 2% six months ago. Since CPI came in at 3% in January, it's a sign that traders are skeptical inflation will make any further progress lower at all this year. That rhymes with the outlook from the Fed, whose officials penciled in 2.5% year-end PCE inflation for 2025 in December. Analysts are expecting PCE inflation to come in at about 2.5% for January--so the Fed estimates too suggest the possibility of no further inflation progress this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1405 ET - The outlook for investment in corporate credit shows some bullish signs, says Dan Genter, of Genter Capital. "There isn't a lot of pressure on earnings...and the momentum is good, cash-flow is strong," he says. His firm traditionally prefers investment-grade credit, but Genter also likes lower-rated corporations likely to be upgraded. He says he usually has 60% of his fixed-income portfolio in corporates and 40% in Treasurys, but it has recently inverted as spreads narrowed to historic lows. "We took profits in some of the individual corporates, where spreads narrowed, and use that to put more into the belly of the curve of the Treasurys." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1402 ET - Trade wars could hurt some sectors that fixed-income investors should avoid, Genter Capital's Dan Genter says. He believes that, eventually, tariffs will be more of a negotiation tool and not as massive as currently feared. But markets will be driven by perception, punishing industries perceived as potential victims of reciprocal tariffs from U.S. trade partners. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1400 ET - Ten-year and longer Treasury yields are likely to rise, Genter Capital's Dan Genter says. He doesn't forecast much inflation as a result of tariffs, while he expects the economy to grow. 1349 ET - Desjardins Group economist Royce Mendes says Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem's speech confirms the BOC would cut rates should President Trump impose 25% tariffs on Canadian nonenergy imports, and 10% levy on Canadian energy products. Mendes cites one sentence in the speech, in which Macklem says that cuts could help smooth the adjustment to tariffs "provided the inflationary impact of tariffs is not too big." Mendes says this is in line with Desjardins' view that BOC would cut its policy rate by a half- to full-percentage point more than otherwise should hefty US tariffs materialize. In a Q&A session with a suburban Toronto audience after his speech, Macklem cautions BOC can't allow a "tariff problem to become an inflation problem." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1046 ET - Treasury yields weaken on signs that the U.S. economy came close to stagnation amid uncertainty about government policies. The S&P Global Flash U.S. PMI Composite Output Index declines to 50.4 this month from 52.7 in January, the weakest level in 17 months. A reading below 50 would indicate contraction. Meanwhile, January existing home sales fell more than expected, contracting 4.9%. An economic slowdown could increase the chances of an interest rate cut by the Fed in the next few months. Bond yields were already sluggish ahead of the data and fell more sharply afterward. The 10-year is at 4.459% and the two-year at 4.241%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1030 ET - Retail spending in Canada is set to slow in 1Q after a strong December. Still, TD Bank economist Maria Solovieva says the underlying fundamentals point to solid domestic consumption in 1Q, barring President Trump following through on his threat of a 25% tariff on all nonenergy products. Solovieva says domestic spending will be buoyed by steady income growth, a resilient labor market, accumulated savings, and a 2-percentage-point drop in interest rates since June of last year. Economists at BMO Capital Markets also back this view, saying a proxy of inflation-adjusted income — with a focus on after-tax wages and hours worked — is up 4% from a year ago, which is the strongest gain in 15 years excluding the Covid-19 pandemic. (paul.vieira@wsj.com)

1027 ET - Consumer spending was clearly improving during 2H 2024, even before the temporary boost in December from the federal tax holiday but recent tariff uncertainty may have resulted in households tightening purse strings, says CIBC Capital Markets' Andrew Grantham. He expects consumer spending to slow in 1H 2025 before accelerating again in 2H and 2026 if a worst case tariff scenario is avoided. Retail sales look to have received a larger-than-expected boost in December from the federal tax holiday, with headline sales rising 2.5% on-month versus the consensus call for 1.6% growth. Grantham says that doesn't look to have carried into January, which marked the first full month of the tax break on some purchases. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1025 ET - Following a 27% devaluation against the dollar in 2024, the Brazilian real is up 8% this year. A report by BNP Paribas says that local funds decreased their BRL short positions to a negative 6.4% on February 17 from a negative 9.3% on January 31. The funds' foreign asset long positioning fell to 2.2% from 6.3%, BNPP says. The bank adds the move coincided with dollar weakness "and the removal of excess premium" after a BRL selloff in December. Over the past year, concerns about increasing government spending have been behind the currency weakness. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1021 ET - Santa had plenty of gifts for Canadians in December, with few apparent worries in the latest retail sales report, BMO reckons. Economist Shelly Kaushik says in a report that even if the sales momentum fades in the new year, the figures add to the argument for the Bank of Canada to pause at next month's policy meeting. Sales rose 2.5% on-month in December, much strongest than expected and the best growth rate since 2022. Sales were also up 2.5% in volume terms, the strongest pace since 2021. Kaushik says while the flash estimate suggests sales fell 0.4% in January, that doesn't retrace December's jump, so markets will need to wait to see if the year-end optimism truly faded. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: ZachXBT identifies Lazarus Group as behind Bybit $1.4B hack, wins Arkham bounty
data.description:
Arkham Intelligence announced that onchain security expert ZachXBT has identified the Lazarus Group, a North Korean hacker group, as behind the $1.46 billion Bybit hack on Feb. 21. Arkham set up a bounty to identify the person or organization behind the attack with a reward of 50,000 ARKM or roughly $31,600.

The Bybit cryptocurrency hack resulted in a loss of $1.46 billion in Ether (ETH) and other ERC-20 tokens. ZachXBT spotted the incident shortly after it occurred and made his submission to Arkham “identifying the organization behind the attack using on-chain data.”

According to Blockaid, an onchain security platform, the $1.46 billion stolen represents the largest crypto exchange hack in history. Given the size and scope of the incident, it was no surprise that the news traveled quickly throughout the crypto community, eliciting reactions ranging from support from other crypto entities and calls to stop the FUD to security advice for users and gallows humor.

Crypto entities post in support of Bybit

In response to the hack, some crypto entities and people expressed support for Bybit. The founder of the Tron blockchain, Justin Sun, said in an X post that the network was assisting in tracking the funds.

Crypto exchange OKX also deployed its security team to support Bybit's investigation, according to its chief marketing officer, Haider Rafique.

The official X account for crypto exchange KuCoin shared a message about the hack, saying it was standing in “full support of Bybit, its team, and CEO Ben Zhou as they work through this challenge.”

KuCoin noted that crypto “is a shared responsibility” and they “firmly believe that collaboration across exchanges is essential in combating cybercrime and strengthening industry-wide security.”

Calls to stop the FUD

As news spread of the hack, some users made calls to stop the fear, uncertainty and doubt (FUD) surrounding the incident, showing community support for Bybit.

Coinbase's executive Conor Grogan wrote on X: “Bybit appears to be processing withdrawals just fine after their hack… They have $20B+ in assets on platform and their cold wallets are untouched. Given the isolated nature of the signing hack and how well capitalized Bybit is, I don’t expect there to be contagion”. He continued:

“A minute into the FTX bankrun it was clear they had no funds to withdraw. I know everyone has PTSD but Bybit is not an FTX situation, if it was I would be screaming it out. They will be fine”.

Stani Kulechov, founder of Aave, which suffered its own large hack, weighed in as well:

Security advice for users

Some members of the crypto community posted security advice for users. Quit, the vice president of Blockchain at Yuga Labs shared on X different security measures users could take to keep their funds safe, including using multisignature, using hardware wallets as signers and running tenderly simulations.

KuCoin also emphasized certain security measures for its users, including enabling two-factor authentication, setting strong, unique passwords, and using passkeys.
